coredata.dc:title: The critical role of surface area optimization in carbon quantum dots modified g-C3N4 for photocatalytic enhancement
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhemi
Chen, Zhongyi
Ji, Tianhao
Jv, Dajian
Guan, Peiyuan
coredata.dc:description: G-C3N4 has shown great potential in photocatalytic degradation. However, the layered structure of g-C3N4 may prevent the contaminant adhesion in the interlayers, which greatly limited its active surface area for photocatalysis. Herein, a simple and fast hydrothermal process was optimized: with appropriate synthesis temperature, carbon dots dispersed into the interlayer of g-C3N4 without destroying the frame structure, opened the layer distance of g-C3N4, enlarged its surface area by 3.56 times, and enhanced its photoresponse and photoredox capacity significantly. Excellent photocatalytic activities were observed towards organic dyes Rhodamine B (RhB). This work provides a new approach to optimize the layered g-C3N4 via surface area modification, which enhanced the design and fabrication of a g-C3N4-based photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanocomposites
Nanocrystalline materials

The authors would like to thank Dr. Joshua Elliot for proofreading this article.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of pure and carbon-dot-modified g-C3N4.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of RhB with pure and carbon-dot-modified g-C3N4; (b) the variation curves of In(Co/Ct) with the reaction time for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB; (c) The kinetic constants of RhB degradation with pure and carbon-dot-modified g-C3N4.

(a) BET surface area of pure and carbon-dot-modified g-C3N4; (b) schematic of the modification of surface area of g-C3N4 via C-dot-modification; (c) photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CC-180 with different scavengers under UV irradiation; (d) proposed mechanism of RhB photodegradation under UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: The critical role of surface area optimization in carbon quantum dots modified g-C3N4 for photocatalytic enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Material Engineering College, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Material Engineering College, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: G-C3N4 has shown great potential in photocatalytic degradation. However, the layered structure of g-C3N4 may prevent the contaminant adhesion in the interlayers, which greatly limited its active surface area for photocatalysis. Herein, a simple and fast hydrothermal process was optimized: with appropriate synthesis temperature, carbon dots dispersed into the interlayer of g-C3N4 without destroying the frame structure, opened the layer distance of g-C3N4, enlarged its surface area by 3.56 times, and enhanced its photoresponse and photoredox capacity significantly. Excellent photocatalytic activities were observed towards organic dyes Rhodamine B (RhB). This work provides a new approach to optimize the layered g-C3N4 via surface area modification, which enhanced the design and fabrication of a g-C3N4-based photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: As a rising star in the carbon nitride family, graphite-C3N4 (g-C3N4) shows great potential in many promising fields, including biosensors, catalysts, and energy storage [1,2]. G-C3N4 promises high photocatalytic activity owing to its outstanding efficient light capture, tunable photoluminescence, and up-conversion photoluminescence properties [3–5]. Despite the aforementioned advantages, the bulk g-C3N4 is still encountering drawbacks. The low quantum efficiency has greatly limited its photocatalytic activity, and its layer-by-layer structure limited the effective surface area in the photocatalytic reactions [6]. To improve its photocatalytic activity, various strategies, including non-metal doping [7,8], heterojunction structure [9], metal deposition [10], morphology control [11], and forming heterostructure with graphene oxide, carbon nanospheres, and carbon dots [12,13], etc., have been developed.

G-C3N4 loaded with carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have been investigated to improve the degradation ability of organic pollutants, as the valence band potential of g-C3N4 is located at 1.57 eV, which is not adequate for the direct OH− oxidation to ·OH, and limited the contaminant degradations [14]. Zhang et al. [15] synthesized C-dots/g-C3N4 (CC) by “dipping-heat”, which enhanced the generation of electron-hole pairs, improved the phenol degradation efficiency. However, most of the works on C-dots-modified g-C3N4 have been focusing on enhancing photocatalytic activities via tuning the generation of·OH or electron-hole pairs, while the layered structure optimization of g-C3N4 has been overlooked.

In this study, we fabricated a high surface area C-dots/g-C3N4 as a more efficient photocatalyst via a one-step hydrothermal method. The photodegradability of the heterostructure has been significantly enhanced owing to its high surface area and good dispersibility. Meanwhile, the enhancing of OH− oxidation has also been observed. This study confirmed the critical role of structure optimization in the C-dot-modified g-C3N4.

The C-dots/g-C3N4 (CC) were fabricated via a one-step hydrothermal impregnation process: 0.1 g of C-dots with 1 g of g-C3N4 were mixed and being ground for 30 min (the C-dot/g-C3N4 ratio was explored from 0.05 to 0.5 g C-dots to 1 g g-C3N4 and the yield of CC peaked at 0.1 g C-dots to 1 g g-C3N4, and thus, the optimized ratio has been employed in this work), following with the hydrothermal treatment at 100, 120, 150, 180, and 200 °C for 20 h, labeled as CC-100, CC-120, CC-150, CC-180, and CC-200, respectively. (The TEM images of synthesized C-dots and g-C3N4 are provided in Fig. S1.) Finally, the CC-X was obtained under vacuum drying after centrifugally washing with water and ethanol several times.

XRD patterns of the CC-x, C-dots and g-C3N4 were provided in Fig.1 (a). The diffraction peaks at ∼ 27.5° and ∼ 13.1° observed from the pure g-C3N4 corresponded to (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) crystal planes of the graphitic structure. The strongest diffraction peak (0 0 2) at 27.7° corresponding to a distance of 0.321 nm reflects the inter-planar stacking of the aromatic system, and the 13.1° peak is derived from the (1 0 0) in-plane repeated the tri-s-triazine units (JCPDS card 87–1526) [16]. No diffraction peaks of C-dots have been observed owing to the relatively low contents. It is noticeable that the intensity of (0 0 2) peaks decreased when synthesis temperature was increased, and that of the CC-200 weakened significantly, indicating the loss of ordered structures within the g-C3N4 framework and the resulting structure is smaller as the (0 0 2) peak becomes wider and weaker. The peaks at 11°of CC-180 and CC-200 confirmed the formation of a new structure, indicating the inserting of C-dots in the g-C3N4.

To further analyze the transition in the C-dot/g-C3N4 heterostructure, FT-IR spectra are compared in Fig. 1(b). The typical g-C3N4 absorption at 1200 to 1700 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibrations of CN heterocycles, and absorption around 3000 cm−1 corresponded to the N–H stretching vibrations [17,18]. The absorption at 810 cm−1 indicated the bending of triazine units of g-C3N4 [17]. The spectra of CC-150 and CC-180 are very similar to that of g-C3N4, indicated the C-dots have not changed the structure of g-C3N4. However, the intensity of C–H, C-N, and C-N-C absorptions of CC-150 and 180 increased significantly, indicating the stronger interaction between C-dots and g-C3N4. For CC-200, all the typical absorptions of g-C3N4 weakened, revealing the loss of ordered structures within the g-C3N4 framework owing to the higher treatment temperature [17]. Thus, the synthesis temperature of C-dots/g-C3N4 should be controlled within 150℃ ∼ 200℃ to form a stable heterostructure and ensure the framework of g-C3N4.

The degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) aqueous solution with pure and C-dot-modified g-C3N4 has been provided in Fig. 2 (a). In the first 30 min, very limit degradation of RhB can be observed, which indicates the stability of RhB in the aqueous solution. Under UV irradiation, CC-180 shows remarkable photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB among all the photocatalysts. The Ct/C0 value of RhB solution with CC-180 decreased rapidly under UV irradiation, while those with g-C3N4 and CC-150 showed lower degradation to ∼ 0.8, and very limited degradation of RhB can be observed in other samples (within 10%).

The RhB photodecomposition curves are well-fitted with pseudo-first-order kinetics in Fig. 2(b). The k values were obtained via In(CO/Ct) = kt. Accordingly, the calculated rate constants are provided in Fig. 2(c). CC-180 shows the highest kinetic constant (∼4 min−1), followed by g-C3N4 and CC-150 (∼1.75 min−1) and then CC-120 and CC-200.

Such results agree well with the FTIR in Fig. 1(b) that CC-180 enhanced the interaction between C-dots and g-C3N4, indicating the well disperse of C-dots in the g-C3N4 structures, which improved the photocatalytic activity. However, higher synthesis temperature triggered the frame structure loss of g-C3N4, which limited the photocatalytic activity. It is assumed that when the synthesis temperature is not high enough for C-dots to disperse into the interlayer of g-C3N4, C-dots will physically adsorb on the surface of layered g-C3N4, which covered the active sites of g-C3N4 and lower the photocatalytic activity.

To confirm the assumption that C-dots can disperse into the layered g-C3N4 with appropriate synthesis temperature, while only physical absorption can be achieved at lower treatment temperature, and the framework structure of g-C3N4 may lose at a higher temperature, the BET measurements were carried out to provide more direct evidence with the surface area. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms measurements have been provided in Fig. 3 (a). CC-180 shows the highest surface area, followed by CC-200 and CC-150, which confirmed that with 180 ℃, the C-dots disperse into the interlayer of g-C3N4, which significantly improved the surface area. However, with 200 ℃, the framework of g-C3N4 was lost, which also enlarged the surface area, but lower the photocatalytic activity. For g-C3N4 and CC-100 to 150, the surface areas remain at the same level, indicating the original g-C3N4 structure remains, which explains the relatively low photocatalytic activities in such systems. The schematic of the C-dot-modified g-C3N4 at different temperatures is provided in Fig. 3(b). The calculated surface area has been provided in Fig. S2, and the surface area of CC-180 is 3.56 times higher than that of g-C3N4. Thus, the optimization of surface area in C-dot-modified g-C3N4 is critical in enhancing photocatalytic activity.

In addition to evaluate the surface area optimization, it is also necessary to verify the mechanism for the photodegradation. The controlled free-radical capture experiments have been studied by introducing a free radical scavenger into the system. As in Fig. 3(c), the additive disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) as an h+ scavenger shows little effect on the photocatalytic efficiency, and tert-butanol (TBA) as an ·OH radical scavenger is slightly more influential. Notably, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) is not suitable here as a scavenger as the inherent color of the p-BQ solution will increase the light transmittance. However, compared to the significant improvements of photodegradation with CC-180, the ·OH radical is not as critical for photocatalysis enhancement compared to the surface area optimization. Based on the above-mentioned results, a plausible RhB photodegradation mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 3(d). The UV irradiation excited electrons in g-C3N4 from the valence band to conduction band and the e− transferred to the C-dots, forming an e− bank, which accelerates the reduction reaction in the photocatalytic process and the h+ on the g-C3N4 promotes the oxidation reaction as illustrated.

The critical role of g-C3N4 surface area optimization in enhancing its photocatalytic activity has been confirmed. By appropriate synthesis parameters applied, the C-dots may disperse into the interlayer of g-C3N4, open up its layer distance, and significantly enlarge its surface area without destroying the framework. The optimized heterostructure enhanced the attachment of dye, promoted OH− oxidation, and improved the light absorption, results in higher photocatalytic efficiency. This work provides new insights to optimize the g-C3N4 and this technique may also be applied to the modifications of other similar layered photocatalysts.
Zhemi Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhongyi Chen: Investigation. Tianhao Ji: Supervision. Dajian Jv: . Peiyuan Guan: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the funding of the Agricultural Environmental Monitoring Station of Beijing (No. 19000531813, 19000551279, and 19000551347) and Chemistry and Material Engineering College in BTBU. The authors would like to thank Dr. Joshua Elliot for proofreading this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.131273.
